MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION As To The Third, Fourth, Fifth And Fourteenth Causes Of Action In The Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-01-2016 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court confirmed its tentative ruling. Cross-defendant Michael Pohl's motion for summary adjudication as to the third, fourth, fifth, and fourteenth causes of action in the cross-complaint is granted as to all four causes of action against cross-complainants Gregory Rossman and Andrew Chase, Trustee of the Andrew Chase 2005 Revocable Trust UAD 3/29/05 as amended 5/10/20 (the Investor Group). In the third cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty, the Investor Group alleges that Mr. Pohl violated his fiduciary duty by issuing a check to himself, failing to provide Mr. Tyszkiewicz material information, and participating in the shareholder meeting where Mr. Draper was removed as a board member. (Cross-complaint par. 101). The Investor Group cannot establish any of these theories of liability. Mr. Pohl never processed or negotiated the $63,000 check. Mr. Nordberg found the check in a box. (Nordberg Depo. 158:3-17). The declaration of Mr. Tyszkiewicz states that he was not misled and Mr. Tyszkiewicz -not the Investor Group-has standing to assert a claim based on the information provided to Mr. Tyszkiewicz. The operative documents show that the Investor Group are holders of convertible debt and they fail to show that Mr. Pohl breached a duty to them. (Chase Dec. Ex. A; Rossmann Dec. Ex. A; Nordberg Dec. Ex. 2. ). A corporation and its directors do not owe a fiduciary duty to its debt holders. (Cleveland v.Johnson (2012) 209 Cal.App.4th 1315, 1342.) The authorities cited by the Investor Group do not change the analysis. GAB Business Services, Inc. v. Lindsey & Newsom Claim Services, Inc. (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 409, 420-42 (disapproved on other grounds in Reeves v. Hanlon (2004) 33 Cal.4th 1140) provides that officers and directors owe a fiduciary duty to the corporation and its shareholders. The Investor Group are suing as debt holders, not shareholders. Berg & Berg Enterprises, LLC v. Boyle (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 1020, 1041 held that directors of an insolvent corporation can owe a duty to a creditor in limited circumstances. The Investor Group fail to show that the Berg duty applies in this case. There is no evidence of asset dissipation or insolvency. Mr. Pohl's undisputed material fact number 38 shows that Mr. Pohl has invested additional funds into Insite since October 31, 2014. Stephenson v. Drever (1997) 16 Cal.4th 1167 merely recites shareholder rights. The Investor Group acknowledges that summary adjudication is proper on the fourth cause of action for conversion and the fifth cause of action for securities fraud. Summary adjudication is also proper on the fourteenth cause of action because injunctive relief is not a "cause of action" and there is no underlying claim to support the requested relief. Order signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-14-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. LISA CHAPMAN, ESQ. of Royse Law Firm, PC, Tel No. 650-813-9700 appearing for Cross-Complainants Gregory Rossman, et al.;
Atty. CHRISTOPHER SARGENT, ESQ. of Computerlaw Group LLP, Tel No. 650-327-9800 appearing for Cross-Defendant Montage Capital, LLC.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court modifies its tentative ruling, and orders as follows: CROSS-DEFENDANT MONTAGE CAPITAL, LLC'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT is sustained without leave in part, and sustained with twenty days leave to amend in part. Defendant's demurrer to the seventh cause of action for unjust enrichment and the fourteenth cause of action for injunctive relief is sustained with twenty days leave to amend. Injunctive relief and unjust enrichment are not causes of action. (McDowell v. Watson (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 1155, 1159; Levine v. Blue Shield of Cal. (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 1117, 1138.) Furthermore, Cross-Complainants' request for unjust enrichment is not based on a quasi-contract or the imposition of a constructive trust. (McKell v. Wash. Atfut, Inc. (2006) 142 Cal.Appl.4th 1457, 1490.) Defendant's demurrer to the first cause of action for misrepresentation, fraud, and deceit and to the second cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is sustained with twenty days leave to amend. Cross-complainants have failed to plead their misrepresentation allegations with the requisite degree of specificity and have not pled sufficient facts to establish that Defendant had a duty to disclose the allegedly concealed information. Defendant's demurrer to the third cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty is sustained with twenty days leave to amend. Cross-Complainants have not alleged that Defendant was a majority shareholder or that Defendant owed Cross-complainants a fiduciary duty. Defendant's demurrer to the fourth cause of action for conversion is sustained, with twenty days leave to amend, to allow Cross-complainants an opportunity to allege facts which support their position that Defendant made fraudulent misrepresentations and which indicate how and when Defendant took possession of the funds. Defendant's demurrer to the sixth cause of action for securities fraud under sections 25110, 25401, 25501, 25503, and 25403 is sustained with twenty leave to amend. Defendant's demurrer to the sixth cause of action for securities fraud under section25504 is sustained, with twenty days leave to amend, so that cross-complainants can plead sufficient facts alleging the existence of a partnership-agency relationship. Defendant's demurrer to the eighth cause of action for a constructive trust is sustained without leave to amend. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)
